plainMeaning: Counting them all together, the Jina-temples in the lower, upper, and middle worlds come to eight crore, fifty-six lakh, ninety-seven thousand, five hundred thirty-four.
recitation: Adho ūrdhva ne tīrcche loke, savve maḷī koḍī āṭhejī. Lākha chappana ne sahasa sattāṇu, paṇasaya cotrīsa pāṭhejī.

wordByWord.etymology: from Skt. adhaḥ 'below'; here qualifying adho-loka, the lower world.
wordByWord.notes: Names the first of the three lokas being summed.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. ūrdhva 'upward, on high'; here the ūrdhva-loka of the heavens.

wordByWord.notes: Joins the third member to the first two.

wordByWord.etymology: from Skt. tiryak / Pkt. tiriccha 'horizontal, sideways'; locative force here = the tiryag-loka.
wordByWord.notes: Spelled તીર્ચ્છે verbatim; denotes the madhya/tiryag-loka completed in the previous verse.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. loka 'world', locative singular loke; here governing all three preceding qualifiers.
wordByWord.notes: One locative covering adho-, ūrdhva- and tiryag-loka together.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. savve from Skt. sarve 'all'; the archaic Prakritic form is retained.
Gujarati maḷvuṁ 'to meet, to be joined'; absolutive maḷī 'having been combined'.
wordByWord.notes: Signals a grand total, not a per-loka figure.
Multiplied by the following 'āṭhe' = eight crore.

wordByWord.etymology: Guj. āṭha 'eight' (here āṭhe) + devotional refrain particle -jī.
wordByWord.notes: -jī is the honorific/emphatic refrain closing every pāda of this dhāla; carries no numerical value.
Links the lakh-count to the thousand-count.

wordByWord.etymology: from Skt. sahasra 'thousand'; Pkt. sahassa, here sahasa.
Pkt. paṇa (Skt. pañca 'five') + saya (Skt. śata 'hundred') = five hundred.
Skt. pāṭha 'text, recitation', locative pāṭhe 'in the text' + refrain -jī.
wordByWord.notes: Asserts the figure is the received/canonical count, 'as stated in the text'.

literalTranslation: In the lower, the upper, and the transverse world — all combined — eight crore, fifty-six lakh, and ninety-seven thousand, five hundred thirty-four, as in the reckoning.
idiomaticTranslation: Taken all together across the lower, the upper, and the middle worlds, the Jina-temples number eight crore, fifty-six lakh, ninety-seven thousand, five hundred and thirty-four — so the scripture reckons them.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse is the cumulative ledger-line of the temple census: where the preceding verse closed the tally of shrines in the transverse (tiryag) world, here the poet draws the three worlds into a single sum — 'savve maḷī' ('all combined') — and announces the grand total of Jina-temples (jinhar/caitya): 8,56,97,534. The structure is purely additive and recited in descending place-values — koḍī (crore), lākha, sahasa, then the hundreds and units — a chanting-friendly numeral cascade in which each place is named before its multiplier (koḍī āṭhe, lākha chappan, sahasa sattāṇu). Two small words carry weight. 'Loke', a single locative, is made to govern all three preceding qualifiers (adho, ūrdhva, tīrcche), so that one breath embraces the whole triloka. 'Pāṭhe' ('in the reckoning/text') stamps the figure as received tradition rather than the poet's estimate — the worshipper is invited to trust, not to audit. The refrain '-jī' on āṭhejī and pāṭhejī is the dhāla's devotional pulse, not a numeral. Note the hinge function: this line completes the count of temples (caitya); the very next verse pivots to the far larger count of Jina-images (pratimā/bimba). So the verse is a structural seam — the moment the hymn finishes 'how many houses of the Jina' before asking 'how many faces of the Jina within them'. The figure given here is a tradition-specific reading; the widely cited Śvetāmbara total of eternal Jina-shrines is 8,57,00,282, so the book's 8,56,97,534 is best treated as this edition's received reckoning and preserved as printed.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the cosmographic doctrine of śāśvata caityas — eternal, uncreated Jina-shrines distributed through the lower, upper, and middle worlds and continuously honoured by gods. To recite their sum is itself an act of dravya-stava (worship through external objects) and a strengthener of samyaktva: by mentally bowing to every shrine at once, the reciter affirms that the Jina is venerated boundlessly, independent of any one earthly temple. The deliberately overwhelming total is a devotional instrument — vastness meant to dissolve the ego's sense of scale, not a sum to be checked.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: When you reach this line, let the number land as awe rather than arithmetic: in one phrase you are bowing to every Jina-temple in all three worlds simultaneously. Chant the place-values steadily, and at 'pāṭhe' rest in trust of the received tradition — a brief, humbling reset before the even greater image-count that follows.

crossReferences.text: Cosmographic counts of śāśvata jina-caityas and bimbas across the three lokas
Preceding verse — tiryag-loka temple tally that this verse totals
crossReferences.source: Kevalnāṇī Bṛhat Caityavandana, Vācaka Mūlā (Anchalgaccha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

sources.title: Śāśvata — definitions (incl. Jainism; Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon)
sources.consultedFor: Sense of śāśvata ('eternal') applied to the everlasting Jina-shrines counted in this verse.

sources.title: Koṭi — definitions (incl. Jainism)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming koḍī = koṭi ('crore'), the highest place-value in the verse's count.

sources.title: Svetambaras — eternal Jina shrines and images in the three lokas
sources.consultedFor: The Śvetāmbara cosmographic totals of śāśvata caityas and bimbas, against which this edition's figure is compared.

notes: The verse gives the combined three-world total of Jina-temples (caitya/jinhar) = 8,56,97,534, read in descending place-values; the immediately following verse switches to the Jina-image (pratimā) count, so this line is the seam between the two censuses. The widely cited Śvetāmbara total of eternal Jina-shrines is 8,57,00,282; the book's 8,56,97,534 is preserved verbatim as this edition's received reckoning and not reconciled. 'āṭhe' and 'pāṭhe' carry the devotional refrain '-jī' (no numeric value). Spellings તીર્ચ્છે, સવ્વે, કોડી kept as printed. Whether the count here is meant as śāśvata-only or as śāśvata+aśāśvata combined is not made explicit in the verse itself.
